title: Wheat
prose: INTRODUCTION Wheat is the most important cereal crop in the world. This is true both for areas planted and total production. The most important wheat producing areas of the world are in the temperate climatic regions of the Northern Hemisphere (30 to 60 °N). The most commonly grown wheat is Triticum aestivum , commonly known as bread wheat. The only other Triticum species of any consequence is T. durum and T. compactum . Of these, only T. durum is grown in South Africa in small quantities for producing macaroni and spaghetti. Wheat is readily adaptable to different climatic conditions, and can be grown successfully from sea level to altitudes of 3 000 m, and with rainfall ranging from 250 to 1 750 mm per annum. Most wheat is grown within a rainfall range of 375 to 875 mm per annum. The minimum temperature is 4 °C, the optimum is 25 °C, and the maximum 30 to 32 °C. Wheat can be grown on most well drained soils. In Africa south of the equator, South Africa and Zimbabwe are the most important wheat producing countries. Wheat production in South Africa for the period 1990 to 2015 is summarised in Table 5.4.3.1. Table 5.4.3.1. Wheat production in the RSA In normal years, local production of wheat is sufficient for domestic consumption. The average yield per hectare has also kept pace over the past decade with a target of 2 tons per hectare by the year 2000. This was already achieved in the 1996 season and again in 1998, 1999 and 2000 (2.52 ton ha -1 ). Whether or not this trend can be sustained is uncertain, as the erratic and generally low rainfall in South Africa means that the production ability of the cultivar will soon reach its limit. The area planted to wheat has also declined considerably, significantly reducing total wheat production. (This necessitates the importation of wheat after poor harvests.) The most important production areas are the Western Cape and the Free State, with various irrigation areas also making a contribution. These areas differ widely in altitude (from sea level to 1 600 m above sea level), latitude (34 and 24 °S), climate (winter and summer rainfall) and soil depth (30 to more than 120 cm). Cultivation practices for optimum yields have been adapted to these contrasting conditions and will be discussed separately for the different areas. PLANT NUTRIENT REQUIREMENTS Average removal figures for N, P, K and S are given in Table 5.4.3.2. Table 5.4.3.2. N, P, K and S removal by wheat per ton of grain 5.4.3.1 SUMMER RAINFALL REGIONS: DRYLAND FREE STATE Wheat is planted in autumn and early winter, from April/May to June/July. During the summer months, cultivation practices are aimed at conserving moisture in the soil, since very little rain can be expected during winter. Soil texture has a major role in determining the moisture conservation practices to be applied, and to ensuring that the maximum moisture is conserved. Clay contents are usually higher in the eastern Free State in comparison with that of the western and north-western Free State. The chances of winter rainfall are also higher in the eastern regions of the Free State. The best conditions for planting or sowing, germination and development of the wheat plant are provided by cultivation practices which not only ensures a good seedbed, but also a subsoil conducive for optimum root development. Cultivars, time of planting, row width and the probability of wind erosion are factors which also influence the choice of cultivation method. The general practice is to band-place fertilizer away from the seed. There are two main reasons for this. Firstly, fertilizer applications have increased greatly over the years, and high application could be detrimental to seed germination. Secondly, mixtures with urea as nitrogen basis are used frequently. Such mixtures can burn the seed and may also harm germination if they are placed close to the seed. Nitrogen (N) FSSA (now known as Fertasa) research from 1978 onwards demonstrated good yield responses to applied-N applications on light textured soils with favourable soil water regimes. Poor or no reactions, on the other hand, were found on the drier Hutton and Bainsvlei soils in the south and south-western Free State. The Small Grain Institute (ARC-SGI) became more involved in research on wheat fertilization after discontinuation of the FSSA research programme. Over time, this research became much more focused because of the effect of nitrogen fertilization on the protein content of the grain. The wheat price is also determined by the protein content. This also brought about a significant change with regard to nitrogen fertilizers. Today, the SGI guidelines are generally used. These guidelines represent guidelines for the different regions and appear in Table 5.4.3.1.1. In the application of these guidelines, the following should be borne in mind: Mono-culture wheat cropping is assumed, and all plant residues incorporated into the soil in good time. All nitrogen fertilization is applied at planting, with no topdressing. All fertilizers are band-placed away from the seed. If pre-plant tillage took place at a late stage and considerable quantities of undecomposed material were still present, nitrogen application can be increased by up to 10 kg ha -1 . Phosphorus (P) A guideline for phosphorus fertilization (ARC-SGI) is given in Table 5.4.3.1.2. Economic principles were applied by the ARC-SGI in compiling the P-guideline. P-application rates in the table are those where gross profits are maximised. Provision is also made for a moderate build-up of the soil-P reserves at low soil test levels. The general practice is to band-place P at planting, using a planter. Potassium (K) Little or no yield increase was found with K-fertilizer applications on wheat. However, where the soil-K status is below 80 mg kg -1 , K-fertilization may be considered. The K-guidelines of the ARC-SGI are given in Table 5.4.3.1.3. pH Even though wheat is not a particularly acid-sensitive crop, it is nevertheless advisable to apply lime, especially if the pH (KCl) drops below 4.8 or if acid saturation is present. OTHER AREAS In the North West and Limpopo provinces, wheat is planted during February and March, and in the Eastern Cape from May to July. Table 5.4.3.1.1. N-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) according to production area and yield target (1) Applicable to the Wesselsbron/Viljoenskroon areas where a high water table and favourable soil water regime allow higher yield targets. (2) West of Humansdorp is considered a winter rainfall area. Table 5.4.3.1.2. P-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) according to yield target and soil- P status (Bray 1 method) * Minimum amount to be applied at low soil-P levels. Table 5.4.3.1.3. Guidelines for K-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) according to texture, K-status and yield target * On soils with > 35% clay. (No K is recommended on soils with < 35% clay.) 5.4.3.2 WINTER RAINFALL REGIONS: DRYLAND WESTERN CAPE Wheat is cultivated in the winter rainfall regions, where rainfall occurs mainly from April to September inclusive. The most important production areas are the Swartland in the western coastal region, where most soils are derived from Malmesbury phyllites and shales, and the southern Cape, most soils are derived from Bokkeveld shales. SWARTLAND SUBREGION Annual rainfall decreases gradually from 500 mm in the south to as low as 200 mm in the north. Approximately 80 to 85% falls during the winter months. The soils derived from the Malmesbury shales are generally shallow (15 to 40 cm) and usually have a clay content of less than 20%. The fine sand plus silt and clay fractions combined often exceed 65%, giving rise to high incidences of soil compaction and surface crusting. Small areas with deeper soils of the Hutton formation and sandy soils overlying a gravel and clay layer also occur. On slopes and poorly drained soils, stubble mulch is being used successfully as a replacement for contours and ridge-and-furrow cultivation. The organic carbon content of the soils is generally in the range of 0.5 and 1%, compared with less than 0.5% for sandy soils. Increasingly, wheat is being used in rotation with canola, medic, oats, korog and lupins. SOUTHERN CAPE SUBREGION In this region, rainfall decreases gradually from approximately 450 mm at Caledon in the west to about 300 mm near Heidelberg and Riversdale in the eastern coastal areas. There is also a gradual decrease in the winter rainfall distribution, from approximately 80% near Caledon to about 60% in the east. The drop in annual rainfall significantly increases the lower proportion of rain that falls during the winter grain production season, with peaks in April and October. Soils that derived from the Bokkeveld shales are similar in depth and texture to those found in the Swartland, although higher gravel and stone fractions are sometimes present. Due to the high proportion of summer rainfall, large areas of dryland lucerne and, to a lesser degree annual medic and subterranean clover, are grown in rotation with cash crops. Wheat accounts for the greatest percentage, with increases in the areas being planted to canola and barley and, to a lesser degree, oats and lupins. The organic carbon content of these soils generally ranges from 1% to 2% and higher. In both subregions, mouldboard- and disc-plough tillage have been replaced mainly by shallower tine and knifepoint tillage. This has resulted from an increased realisation of the advantages of applying mulch/conservation tillage practices. Chemical agents are used as the main form of disease, pest and weed control. Topdressing is applied, and at times there is also a broadcast application. In most instances, different kinds of planters are used. Fertilizer formulations are marketed in bulk, in semi-bulk 500 kg bags, and in 50 kg bags. SOIL ACIDITY REQUIREMENTS FOR WINTER GRAINS Whilst winter cereals are adapted to a wide range in soil pH there are certain minimum requirements for optimal production. Table 5.4.3.2.1. Recommended soil acidity norms PHOSPHORUS (P) (1) Lime is applied according to Eksteen's R-value concept (1969, 1973 â”€ see Chapter 1.8). If the Ca:Mg ratio (centimol) is less than 10 use calcitic lime, otherwise use dolomitic lime. NITROGEN (N) Nitrogen fertilizer guidelines are determined mainly by rainfall, crop production potential, soil texture, winter crop type, crop rotation system and production methods. As a point of departure, guidelines for the N requirements of wheat in kg N ha -1 (N1) grown on stubble with a shallow tine cultivation, are presented in Table 5.4.3.2.2. The nitrogen quantity (N1) as per Table 5.4.3.2.2 can generally be divided, into about 25 – 30 kg ha -1 at planting or seed-sow and the balance in one or two, or even three, equal applications as a topdressing – depending on growth potential and rainfall. Guidelines for adjusting N1 (Approximately 27 kg N t -1 of wheat, plus straw) i) Production record. Adjust the N guideline by 15 kg h -1 for every 500 kg yield deviation according to local production records. ii) Soil texture. The N guideline should be increased by 10 to 15% for sandy soils and decreased by 10 to 15% for clayey soils. iii) Small grain types. Fertilize triticale as for wheat, oats or barley should be given 65 to 70% of the requirement of wheat. Table 5.4.3.2.2. Guidelines for N-fertilization according to rainfall and target yield in the Swartland/Western Cape and Southern Cape (stubble field, shallow tine tillage, kg N ha -1 = N1) Note: Keep rotation crops free from grass. iv) Crop rotation/tillage system. Adjust N1 in Table 5.4.3.2.2 as follows: Band-placement and the splitting of nitrogen applied to wheat. There are no general fertilizer guidelines applicable to all types of planters or all fertilizer blends. Every production environment will require different aspects of N carriers to be considered. These include salt indices, soil moisture, soil texture, row width used when placing the seed, and the amount of crop residue. When the seedbed is dry, applications of more than 12 -14 kg ha -1 urea-N near seed can subsequently damage the seedlings, whereas under moist soil conditions, 25-30 kg ha -1 is considered safe â€“ placement should preferably be 5cm below and 5cm away from the seed. These figures will be significantly higher when LAN is used instead of urea. Some planters allow for N fertilizer to be separated, so that a portion of the N and P is placed close to the seed. The quantity of N being applied next to the seed must not exceed 10 kg ha -1 . N-quantities exceeding 25-40 or even 50 kg ha -1 may be band-placed at planting, provided the application is split and that a portion of it is placed 10-15 cm below the seed. Normally it is recommended that when nitrogen topdressing is used to rectify low yields, application is done within 55-65 days post-emergence. In the case of yields exceeding 4 ton ha -1 , an additional 30 kg ha -1 can be considered for application as late as 80-85 days after the plants have emerged and provided that moisture conditions are favourable. During long-term conservation tillage, the possibility exists that chemical stratification can be problematic. Continually high nitrogen levels can also increase topsoil acidity. Conservation tillage improves the moisture retention and fertility of the soil in the Southern Cape. With the benefits of improved soil fertility, the fertilizer level (N1) recommended in Table 5.4.3.2.2 for both the Swartland and the Southern Cape, can be reduced. As the yield differences between the Swartland and the Southern Cape (Table 5.4.3.2.2) improve, the program for the Southern Cape can gradually be adjusted. SULPHUR (S) Sulphur, alongside calcium and magnesium, is regarded as a secondary nutrient. Sulphur is, in the same way as nitrogen, an essential requirement for the production of amino acids and proteins. Movements, both worldwide and locally, towards producing more highly concentrated fertilizers with low levels of S have led to as much as a 50% reduction in sulphur application levels in the Western Cape since 1989. As a result, and in combination with high levels of grain production, particularly on soils with a carbon content of < 1%, an increase in S deficiency symptoms in grain crops has been observed. Consequently, there is an increasing trend of including S (as sulphate) in fertilizer programmes in the Western Cape. Soil analyses showing less than 10 mg kg -1 (calcium phosphate extraction), leaf analyses of < 0.15% and a soil organic carbon content of < 1% are used as indicators that S should be included in the fertilizer programme. Table 5.4.3.2.3. Guidelines for S-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) Â PHOSPHORUS (P) Phosphorus fertilizer requirements for winter cereal are determined largely by yield potential and soil analyses. Weak acid extraction methods such as the well-known Bray 1, may be neutralised in soils with an inherent pH higher than 6. The Bray 1 method is thus best suited to acid soils, and the Olsen method (weak alkaline) to soils with an inherent pH higher than 6 (KCI), while the citric acid method can be used in the Western Cape for a wide range of pH soils. Unfortunately, the citric acid method is no longer being readily used in commercial laboratories. Research conducted by Thompson (1988, Elsenburg) sheds light on the proportional relationship between methods of analysis and the degree to which varying pH-conditions may have an effect on the extractable-P. Where different P-extraction methods are used in the same production areas, the data given in Table 5.4.3.2.4 indicate how values can vary depending on the different extraction methods used. Because variances are also influenced by factors other than the pH, care must be taken to avoid generalisations. The extraction method being requested should correspond with the one used in calibration studies. The same guidelines can be applied to other winter cereals. The applications are generally combined with nitrogen and are done before or during sowing or planting. Table 5.4.3.2.4. The effect of soil pH (KCl) on phosphorous extraction when using different methods Source: The effect of pH on the assessment of soil P in a range of soils using five different extractants – G.R. Thompson, Elsenburg. (International P symposium, Pretoria, 1988) * Source: C.P. Beyers Nitrophoska 2007 (more than 200 comparable extractions in the Southern Cape, Eastern Cape, Upington and Namibia). Confirmed by Elsenburg, Bemlab and SGS Laboratories. Table 5.4.3.2.5. P-fertilizer per ton recommendations for winter cereals * Should the P quantity band-placed with planters exceed 20 kg ha -1 , then 50% of the application must be done as a corrective measure. POTASSIUM (K) Potassium fertilizer recommendations are also based on soil analysis using the citric acid extraction method (citric acid K is comparable to exchangeable- or Bray extractable K). With the exception of sandy soils, K-analyses of lower than 60 mg kg -1 are seldom recorded for soils of the Western Cape. Even where the soil analyses show low K levels, grain yield increases of more than 10 to 15% are seldom obtained with K supplementary fertilizer. Conversely, while some analyses can appear to be high, the K is not readily accessible and therefore band-placement of K is still recommended. Consequently, local knowledge and experience are imperative when making decisions. In addition, take note of the soil analyses, to ensure that K-levels do not drop too low. The guidelines for K-fertilizer are given in Table 5.4.3.2.6. Table 5.4.3.2.6. Guidelines for K-fertilization (kg ha -1 ) taking rainfall and soil analysis into account (1) Low (2) High The production of straw removes approximately 18 â”€ 20 kg potassium per ton and consequently this must be borne in mind over the longer term. MICRO-ELEMENTS Based on the EDTA extraction method for Cu, Zn and Mn, and hot water extraction for B, the following guidelines are used for cereal crops in the Western Cape. Table 5.4.3.2.7. Soil analysis norms for micro-elements Source: Omnia guidelines for the Western Cape. Soil applications can prevent micro-element deficiencies for longer than 5 years. The efficiency of foliar application decreases if applied later than 35 to 40 days after emergence. Table 5.4.3.2.8. Table 5.4.3.2.8. Applications to control deficiencies (kg ha -1 element) in inorganic saline form Molybdenum deficiencies sometimes occur at a pH (KCl) of about 4.5, and wheat responds well to foliar applications of 150 g sodium molybdate h -1 . Iron deficiencies, which occur less frequently than molybdenum deficiencies, respond well to foliar applications of iron sulphate (0.5â”€1 kg element h -1 in an inorganic form). ACKNOWLEDGEMENT Information supplied is based mainly on research results and practical application thereof in the Western Cape by the University of Stellenbosch, the Department of Agriculture â”€ Elsenburg (Research and Extension) as well as fertilizer companies and agricultural co-operatives. Compiled by Mr. C. P. Beyers, formerly with Elsenburg 1954–1997 and currently with Nitrophoska. 5.4.3.3 UNDER IRRIGATION Fertilization constitutes a significant part of the total production cost under irrigation. It is therefore important to manage this production input at an optimum level. Fertilizer planning for each field should be done on the basis of a feasible yield target. The guidelines given here serve as a point of departure that can be used for planning a fertilizer programme under a given set of circumstances. Nitrogen and irrigation management are two proven factors that can increase the yield and quality of grain under irrigation. In a grain marketing system where quality is of major importance, both these management factors must be kept in mind. Ever-increasing input costs, particularly fertilizer products, continue to put pressure on the profitability of wheat production. Although general field and crop management practices determine successful production, there are certain factors which can influence crop yield, quality, and the optimum efficacy of fertilizer application. The choice of cultivar, alongside seeding density and decisions regarding fertilizer management, are among the factors that play a major role in the successful production of wheat under irrigation. DETERMINATION OF YIELD POTENTIAL The determination of a realistic yield potential (yield target) is important in planning a fertilizer programme. This yield, in conjunction with soil analysis, determines the quantity of fertilizer and nutrients to be applied, leading to financially viable crop production. The yield target is determined by a number of factors, including the following: Experience: historic average yield. Amount of rain and irrigation applied. Choice of cultivar and date of planting. Crop rotation system. Farmers should be aware of factors that may have a positive or negative effect on yield potential. The risk associated with a determined yield target should be realistically considered. While profit is the compensation for risks taken, it should be borne in mind that certain management practices have a high inherent risk component. ARC-SGI grain production manuals contain valuable guidelines for farmers as a reference when planning a fertilizer program. Manuals for 2017 are available. NITROGEN A guideline for nitrogen fertilization appears in Table 5.4.3.3.1. This guideline is based on the requirements of plants at different yield levels. Table 5.4.3.3.1. N-fertilization (kg N ha -1 ) under irrigation according to yield target The guidelines in Table 5.4.3.3.1 do not make provision for a crop rotation practice with wheat after legumes. Because legumes have the ability to fix atmospheric nitrogen in plant-available form, a downward adjustment of fertilizer N-requirement is often justified. Conversely, if large quantities of crop residues are incorporated shortly before planting, upward adjustment may be necessary. Consult with an expert in such eventualities. The practice of splitting nitrogen applications under irrigation has received much attention over the years. It has been found that effective nitrogen management throughout the growing season enables farmers to obtain high grain yields with acceptable protein content. Furthermore, adjustments in higher yield potential due to cooler winter conditions, coupled with increased tillering can also be easily managed. The rationale for splitting N-applications during the growing season is to increase the effectiveness of N-uptake when most required for growth and development. A schedule for splitting nitrogen applications at different yield targets on a soil with 15 to 25% clay content is given in Table 5.4.3.3.2. On soils with a lower clay percentage, the quantities applied at planting and at tillering may be further subdivided depending upon practical circumstances for the prevention of crop losses. On soils with a higher clay percentage (> 25%), the same split as indicated in Table 5.4.3.3.2 may be followed. The importance of effective irrigation scheduling and water management cannot be over-emphasised, since these ultimately determine the effectiveness of nitrogen fertilization and the splitting of N-applications. Table 5.4.3.3.2. Splitting of N-applications over the growing season at different yield levels Irrigated wheat that falls short of the required grade is one of the most pressing problems facing farmers who plant wheat under irrigation. Owing to the high yields obtainable under irrigation, there is always a risk that grain protein levels could drop too low. It is imperative that producers pay close attention to managing this aspect. Low protein values in wheat can be ascribed to various factors – climatic conditions, especially rainfall and temperature, play a decisive role because of their combined effect on yield. Favourable environmental conditions result in higher yield levels, with accompanying lower protein levels in most cases. Conversely, water stress has the opposite effect, namely a lower yield and a rise in the wheat protein level. Hence, the combination of yield obtained and nitrogen applied is important, as N-applications that have been too low for the grain yield result in lower protein values. Research has shown that efficient nitrogen management throughout the growing season enables a farmer to achieve a high grain yield with an acceptable protein level. If the yield potential rises as a result of cooler winter conditions, accompanied by increased tillering this change can still be easily managed. Trials were conducted at the Vaalharts, Riet River, Koedoeskop and Loskop irrigation schemes to determine the effect of split nitrogen applications on the growth, yield and grain protein of wheat. In all the trials, the protein content of the wheat increased by between 1% and 1.5% with an application of 20 to 40 kg N ha -1 during the flag leaf to head emergence stages. Yield also improved with the splitting of the nitrogen fertilizer. Nitrogen management up to the late piping stage of wheat development is aimed at increasing yield potential, while applications during the later growth stages (flag leaf to flowering) primarily improve the quality of the grain. The trials also proved that acceptable grain content can be achieved in these irrigation areas. Despite higher yields, the protein percentage can be managed by appropriate, corrective N-applications. In situations where the nitrogen supply is restrictive (higher yield potential which follows cooler winter conditions and the accompanying increase in tillering, the crop prioritises the realisation of yield potential, as determined by environmental conditions, to the detriment of the protein content of the grain. This situation creates a risk factor: if the yield potential should exceed the initial level planned for, the nitrogen already applied will be insufficient to meet the requirements necessary for acceptable grain protein levels. Therefore, the reduction in grain protein produced will result in a lower crop grading, with concomitant financial losses for the producer. It is essential that nitrogen management and yield potential conditions are evaluated during the critical crop development stages – jointing to flag leaf. Effective nitrogen management at this time can ensure yield potential but, more importantly, also ensure that the protein content of the grain is increased to acceptable levels. Research has shown that higher N-applications lead to higher grain protein levels. This indicates that it is a workable management option in situations where nitrogen is the limiting factor. Additional adjustments regarding higher yield potential as a result of cooler winters, coupled with higher tillering, are then easier to manage. The first step is to determine the yield potential of every block of wheat under irrigation, by counting the stalks and/or ears per square metre. With an estimated number of grains per ear and grain weight, a yield in tons per hectare can be calculated. With this figure and the nitrogen fertilizer guidelines in mind, nitrogen recommendations can be established and evaluated. In the event of yield levels rising to such an extent that a nitrogen deficiency can be expected, adjustments must be made. Yellowing plants, a die-back in the lowest and oldest leaves, or plants that show an overall pale green colour, are indications of a moderate to serious nitrogen deficiency. In addition, the application of nitrogen fertilizer during the flag leaf to flowering stadia of plant development is important in order to ensure that sufficient nitrogen is available for kernel growth and development, as well as to ensure acceptable grain protein levels. Depending on yield potential, between 30 and 60 kg ha -1 should be applied in order to increase the grain protein level to above 11%. Nitrogen is applied during the abovementioned period in order to ensure that plants have sufficient time for optimum nutrient uptake and metabolism. In irrigation farming, the management of both nitrogen and water go hand in hand. It is thus important to ensure that there is sufficient water in the soil profile to prevent moisture stress and to ensure the effective uptake of nutrients. Water should only be withheld when the peduncle has lost its green colour. During this stage of the growing season, the plant has already started transporting nutrients to the developing grain and, consequently, water stress will hamper grain development and result in low hectolitre mass. PHOSPHORUS In interpreting the phosphorus fertilizer guidelines for wheat under irrigation, the following should be borne in mind: When referring to phosphorus fertilization, citric acid or water-soluble phosphorus sources are implied. Economic principles were applied in drawing up the guidelines, and therefore the P-application rates are those where profits will be maximised. The guidelines also make provision for a moderate build-up of the soil-P reserves where soil-P test levels are low and the wheat straw is not removed. A gradual process of building up soil-P reserves is preferred. Higher P-application rates in the guidelines would correspond to lower soil-P test levels and vice versa. For soil test levels between these limits, the correct P-application rates should be deduced by interpolation. Laboratories use a variety of methods for soil-P analysis. Since the analysis values obtained with the different methods may differ substantially, it is necessary to be able to make a reasonable comparison between the various methods. Although the different methods used on clayey soils (black turf) and strong acidic or alkaline soils do not maintain constant ratios under all conditions, the approximate proportionate relationships appear in Table 5.4.3.2.4, while Table 5.4.3.3.3 gives phosphorus fertilization application rates for wheat under irrigation at different yield targets and soil-P quality according to the Bray 1 analysis method. Table 5.4.3.3.3. P-application rates (kg P ha -1 ) under irrigation at different yield targets and soil phosphorus quality according to Bray 1 analysis method POTASSIUM Potassium deficiencies are not common in wheat, since South African soils in general are rich in potassium. Consequently, farmers seldom achieve higher yields through K-applications. Conditions where potassium shortages can occur are the following: highly-leached sandy soils with an inherently low K-content, cold and/or wet weather, and/or very dry conditions, as well as soils with a very high magnesium and/or calcium content, prevalent in many of the fields under irrigation. Guidelines for K-fertilization of wheat under irrigation according to the soil potassium status and yield target appear in Table 5.4.3.3.4. The potassium can be mixed with nitrogen and phosphorus, applied as a pre-plant broadcast, and then worked into the soil. Table 5.4.3.3.4. Guidelines for K-fertilization (kg K ha -1 ) under irrigation according to soil-K status and yield target * On soils with < 35% clay (on soils > 35% clay no potassium is recommended). Note: With high Ca- and/or Mg-content of soils, an upward adjustment of the K-guideline may be considered. The same applies to highly leached sandy soils with low inherent soil-K values. Producers must plan the fertilizer input for their envisaged irrigated wheat crop and ensure that the appropriate fertilizer volumes will be applied for the correct reasons. With nitrogen management practice, it is particularly important to interpret and understand the effect of climate conditions during the growing season, as well as the yield and the protein content of the grain, so that adjustments can be made in good time. Crop management during the growing season includes the full spectrum of practices that can affect the growth and development of the crop. In the light of the new marketing system, these aspects are particularly important for wheat farmers. It is now possible to assist wheat farmers with recommendations for the appropriate nitrogen volumes and split application rates required in order to achieve yield potential while ensuring acceptable ACKNOWLEDGEMENT The input of the ARC-SGI for the content of this chapter is gratefully acknowledged, with special thanks to Messrs. Willem Otto and Kobus van Zyl.
tables: Eastern Cape Coastal Area (east of Humansdorp) (2)
Method as affected by soil pH classes (KCI)
Citric acid phosphorous (mg kg -1 ) as a reference method NOT pH sensitive
Bray 1 (0.025 M. HCl + 0.03 M. NH 4 F) - weak acid. Method developed for acid soils. Sensitivity decreases progressively at pH > 6.0.
Bray 2 (0.1 M. HCl + 0.03 M. NH 4 F) - higher acidity than Bray 1. Sensitivity decreases at pH > 6.9.
Mehlich 3 (0.2 M. CH 3 COOH + 0.25 M. NH 4 NO 3 + 0.015 M. NH 4 F + 0.13 M. HNO 3 + 0.001 M. EDTA). Sensitivity decreases progressively at pH > 6.0.
ISFEI (AMBIC) (0.05 M. NH 4 HCO 3 + 0.01 M. NH 4 F + 0.01 M. EDTA). Because of bicarbonate reagent, is often confused with Olsen.
* Olsen method (0.05 M. NaHCO 3 ) for soil with pH > 6.0 (KCl) or > 7.0 (water). Poor sensitivity of soils in the Western Cape.
